name: Hydrogen

image.title: Vial of glowing ultrapure hydrogen, H2. Original size in cm: 1 x 5
image.attribution: User:Jurii, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, source: https://images-of-elements.com/hydrogen.php

funFacts: Hydrogen is the most abundant element in the universe, making up about 75% of all normal matter by mass.
A single hydrogen atom is so small that 10 million of them lined up would only be 1 millimeter wide.
Hydrogen fuel cells convert hydrogen directly into electricity, with pure water as the only byproduct.
summary: Hydrogen is a chemical element with chemical symbol H and atomic number 1. With an atomic weight of 1.00794 u, hydrogen is the lightest element on the periodic table. Its monatomic form (H) is the most abundant chemical substance in the Universe, constituting roughly 75% of all baryonic mass.

name: Helium
appearance: colorless gas, exhibiting a red-orange glow when placed in a high-voltage electric field

image.title: Vial of glowing ultrapure helium. Original size in cm: 1 x 5
image.attribution: Jurii, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, source: https://images-of-elements.com/helium.php

funFacts: Helium was discovered in the Sun before it was found on Earth — its name comes from Helios, the Greek sun god.
Helium is the only element that cannot be solidified by cooling alone; it requires extreme pressure as well.
The United States holds the world's largest helium reserve, stored underground in a natural gas field in Texas.
summary: Helium is a chemical element with symbol He and atomic number 2. It is a colorless, odorless, tasteless, non-toxic, inert, monatomic gas that heads the noble gas group in the periodic table. Its boiling and melting points are the lowest among all the elements.
discoveryCountry: United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland

name: Lithium

image.title: 0.5 Grams Lithium under Argon. Original size of the largest piece in cm: 0.3 x 4
image.attribution: Hi-Res Images ofChemical Elements, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, source: https://images-of-elements.com/lithium.php

funFacts: Lithium is so light it can float on water — and it's only about half the density of water.
Lithium carbonate has been used since the 1940s as a mood stabilizer for bipolar disorder.
The lithium-ion battery in your phone contains only a few grams of lithium but can store enormous amounts of energy.
summary: Lithium (from Greek:λίθος lithos, "stone") is a chemical element with the symbol Li and atomic number 3. It is a soft, silver-white metal belonging to the alkali metal group of chemical elements. Under standard conditions it is the lightest metal and the least dense solid element.

name: Beryllium

image.title: Pure Beryllium bead, 2.5 grams. Original size in cm: 1 x 1.5
image.attribution: Hi-Res Images ofChemical Elements, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, source: https://images-of-elements.com/beryllium.php

funFacts: Beryllium is transparent to X-rays, making it the ideal material for X-ray tube windows in medical equipment.
Emeralds and aquamarines are both beryllium minerals — beryl (Be₃Al₂Si₆O₁₈) gives them their color.
Beryllium is incredibly stiff — it's six times stiffer than steel at only one-quarter the weight.
summary: Beryllium is a chemical element with symbol Be and atomic number 4. It is created through stellar nucleosynthesis and is a relatively rare element in the universe. It is a divalent element which occurs naturally only in combination with other elements in minerals.

name: Boron

image.title: Pure Crystalline Boron, front and back side. Original size in cm: 2 x 3
image.attribution: Jurii, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, source: https://images-of-elements.com/boron.php

funFacts: Boron is the only non-metal in Group 13 and behaves more like a semiconductor than a typical element.
Borax, a boron compound, has been used as a cleaning agent for over 100 years.
The hardest material after diamond — cubic boron nitride — is used to cut and grind hardened steel.
summary: Boron is a metalloid chemical element with symbol B and atomic number 5. Produced entirely by cosmic ray spallation and supernovae and not by stellar nucleosynthesis, it is a low-abundance element in both the Solar system and the Earth's crust. Boron is concentrated on Earth by the water-solubility of its more common naturally occurring compounds, the borate minerals.
discoveryCountry: United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland

name: Carbon

image.title: Element 6 - Carbon
image.attribution: Texas Lane, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

funFacts: Carbon forms more compounds than all other elements combined — over 10 million known organic compounds.
Diamond and graphite are both pure carbon, yet diamond is the hardest natural material while graphite is soft enough to write with.
Carbon-14 radioactive dating can accurately determine the age of organic materials up to about 50,000 years old.
summary: Carbon (from Latin:carbo "coal") is a chemical element with symbol C and atomic number 6. On the periodic table, it is the first (row 2) of six elements in column (group) 14, which have in common the composition of their outer electron shell. It is nonmetallic and tetravalent—making four electrons available to form covalent chemical bonds.

name: Nitrogen

image.title: Vial of Glowing Ultrapure Nitrogen, N2. Original size in cm: 1 x 5
image.attribution: Jurii, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, source: https://images-of-elements.com/nitrogen.php

funFacts: Nitrogen makes up 78% of Earth's atmosphere — yet most living things cannot use it directly from the air.
Liquid nitrogen at −196°C is used to freeze and preserve biological samples, food, and even human tissue.
The nitrogen in your body's DNA and proteins was once 'fixed' from the atmosphere by soil bacteria.
summary: Nitrogen is a chemical element with symbol N and atomic number 7. It is the lightest pnictogen and at room temperature, it is a transparent, odorless diatomic gas. Nitrogen is a common element in the universe, estimated at about seventh in total abundance in the Milky Way and the Solar System.

name: Oxygen

image.title: Liquid Oxygen in a Beaker
image.attribution: Staff Sgt. Nika Glover, U.S. Air Force, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

funFacts: Oxygen was independently discovered by Carl Wilhelm Scheele and Joseph Priestley in the 1770s, but Antoine Lavoisier named it.
Almost all of Earth's breathable oxygen was produced by photosynthetic life over billions of years.
Liquid oxygen is pale blue and strongly magnetic — it will stick to a magnet.
summary: Oxygen is a chemical element with symbol O and atomic number 8. It is a member of the chalcogen group on the periodic table and is a highly reactive nonmetal and oxidizing agent that readily forms compounds (notably oxides) with most elements. By mass, oxygen is the third-most abundant element in the universe, after hydrogen and helium.

name: Fluorine

image.title: Liquid Fluorine at -196°C
image.attribution: Fulvio314, CC BY-SA 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

funFacts: Fluorine is the most electronegative and most reactive of all elements — it reacts violently with almost everything.
Teflon (PTFE), the non-stick coating on cookware, is a carbon-fluorine compound so stable it's nearly inert.
Fluoride in drinking water and toothpaste has dramatically reduced tooth decay rates worldwide since the 1940s.
summary: Fluorine is a chemical element with symbol F and atomic number 9. It is the lightest halogen and exists as a highly toxic pale yellow diatomic gas at standard conditions. As the most electronegative element, it is extremely reactive:almost all other elements, including some noble gases, form compounds with fluorine.

name: Neon
appearance: colorless gas exhibiting an orange-red glow when placed in a high voltage electric field

image.title: Vial of Glowing Ultrapure neon. Original size in cm: 1 x 5
image.attribution: Jurii, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, source: https://images-of-elements.com/neon.php

funFacts: Neon signs don't actually use neon for most colors — only red-orange light comes from real neon; other colors use different gases.
Neon is the fifth most abundant element in the universe but extremely rare on Earth.
Despite its cosmic abundance, neon was not discovered until 1898 because it is completely inert and colorless.
summary: Neon is a chemical element with symbol Ne and atomic number 10. It is in group 18 (noble gases) of the periodic table. Neon is a colorless, odorless, inert monatomic gas under standard conditions, with about two-thirds the density of air.

name: Sodium

image.title: Na (Sodium) Metal
image.attribution: The original uploader was Dnn87 at English Wikipedia., CC BY-SA 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

funFacts: Sodium explodes dramatically when dropped in water — it reacts so fast it ignites the hydrogen gas produced.
Your body contains about 100 grams of sodium, essential for nerve impulses and muscle contractions.
Table salt (NaCl) is so fundamental to human history that the word 'salary' derives from the Latin word for salt.
summary: Sodium /ˈsoʊdiəm/ is a chemical element with symbol Na (from Ancient Greek Νάτριο) and atomic number 11. It is a soft, silver-white, highly reactive metal. In the Periodic table it is in column 1 (alkali metals), and shares with the other six elements in that column that it has a single electron in its outer shell, which it readily donates, creating a positively charged atom - a cation.

name: Magnesium

image.title: Magnesium crystals
image.attribution: Warut Roonguthai, CC BY-SA 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

funFacts: Magnesium burns with such an intense white flame that it was used in early photography as flash powder.
Chlorophyll — the molecule that makes plants green and drives photosynthesis — has magnesium at its center.
Magnesium alloys are used in aircraft and racing cars because they are the lightest structural metal.
summary: Magnesium is a chemical element with symbol Mg and atomic number 12. It is a shiny gray solid which bears a close physical resemblance to the other five elements in the second column (Group 2, or alkaline earth metals) of the periodic table:they each have the same electron configuration in their outer electron shell producing a similar crystal structure. Magnesium is the ninth most abundant element in the universe.

name: Aluminium

image.title: Pure aluminium foil. Original size in cm: 5 x 5
image.attribution: Jurii, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, source: https://images-of-elements.com/aluminium.php

funFacts: Aluminum was once more valuable than gold — Napoleon III reserved aluminum cutlery for honored guests, giving lesser guests gold ones.
Aluminum is the most abundant metal in Earth's crust, making up about 8% of it by mass.
Recycling one aluminum can saves enough energy to power a TV for 3 hours.
summary: Aluminium (or aluminum; see different endings) is a chemical element in the boron group with symbol Al and atomic number 13. It is a silvery-white, soft, nonmagnetic, ductile metal. Aluminium is the third most abundant element (after oxygen and silicon), and the most abundant metal, in the Earth's crust.

name: Silicon

image.title: Chunk of Ultrapure Silicon, 2 x 2 cm
image.attribution: Jurii, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, source: https://images-of-elements.com/silicon.php

funFacts: Silicon is the foundation of modern computing — every microchip and transistor is built from silicon.
Silicon is the second most abundant element in Earth's crust, after oxygen.
Sand and glass are both largely made of silicon dioxide (SiO₂) — we literally build computers from sand.
summary: Silicon is a chemical element with symbol Si and atomic number 14. It is a tetravalent metalloid, more reactive than germanium, the metalloid directly below it in the table. Controversy about silicon's character dates to its discovery.

name: Phosphorus
appearance: colourless, waxy white, yellow, scarlet, red, violet, black

image.title: Purple Phosphorus
image.attribution: Jurii, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, source: https://images-of-elements.com/phosphorus.php

funFacts: White phosphorus glows in the dark when exposed to oxygen — the word 'phosphorus' means 'light-bringer' in Greek.
Phosphorus is essential for DNA and ATP — without it, no life on Earth could store or transfer energy.
The first element ever isolated by a chemist was phosphorus, accidentally discovered by Hennig Brand in 1669 from urine.
summary: Phosphorus is a chemical element with symbol P and atomic number 15. As an element, phosphorus exists in two major forms—white phosphorus and red phosphorus—but due to its high reactivity, phosphorus is never found as a free element on Earth. Instead phosphorus-containing minerals are almost always present in their maximally oxidised state, as inorganic phosphate rocks.

name: Sulfur

image.title: Native Sulfur From Russia
image.attribution: James St. John, CC BY 2.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

funFacts: Sulfur is mentioned in the Bible as 'brimstone' and has been known since ancient times — it was used to fumigate homes.
The smell of rotten eggs comes from hydrogen sulfide (H₂S), which sulfur-reducing bacteria produce.
Sulfuric acid is the most widely produced industrial chemical in the world, used in fertilizers, batteries, and refining.
summary: Sulfur or sulphur (see spelling differences) is a chemical element with symbol S and atomic number 16. It is an abundant, multivalent non-metal. Under normal conditions, sulfur atoms form cyclic octatomic molecules with chemical formula S8.

name: Chlorine

image.title: A Sample of Chlorine
image.attribution: Benjah-bmm27, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

funFacts: Chlorine was used as a chemical weapon in World War I — it was the first large-scale use of a chemical agent in warfare.
Chlorine in swimming pools doesn't just kill bacteria — it reacts with urine and sweat to produce the irritating 'pool smell.'
PVC (polyvinyl chloride) plastic, used in pipes, flooring, and clothing, is made from chlorine and ethylene.
summary: Chlorine is a chemical element with symbol Cl and atomic number 17. It also has a relative atomic mass of 35.5. Chlorine is in the halogen group (17) and is the second lightest halogen following fluorine.

name: Argon
appearance: colorless gas exhibiting a lilac/violet glow when placed in a high voltage electric field

image.title: Vial of glowing ultrapure argon. Original size in cm: 1 x 5
image.attribution: Jurii, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, source: https://images-of-elements.com/argon.php

funFacts: Argon makes up about 1% of Earth's atmosphere, making it the third most abundant atmospheric gas.
Argon is used to fill the space between double-pane windows because it is a poor conductor of heat.
The name 'argon' comes from the Greek word for 'lazy' or 'inactive' — reflecting its complete chemical inertness.
summary: Argon is a chemical element with symbol Ar and atomic number 18. It is in group 18 of the periodic table and is a noble gas. Argon is the third most common gas in the Earth's atmosphere, at 0.934% (9,340 ppmv), making it over twice as abundant as the next most common atmospheric gas, water vapor (which averages about 4000 ppmv, but varies greatly), and 23 times as abundant as the next most common non-condensing atmospheric gas, carbon dioxide (400 ppmv), and more than 500 times as abundant as the next most common noble gas, neon (18 ppmv).
discoveryCountry: United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland

name: Potassium

image.title: Potassium Pieces
image.attribution: Dnn87, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

funFacts: Potassium's symbol K comes from 'kalium,' its Latin name, because it was first isolated from potash (wood ash).
Bananas are famously high in potassium, but you'd need to eat about 10 million of them to get a lethal dose.
Potassium-40 is naturally radioactive — your own body contains enough to be slightly radioactive.
summary: Potassium is a chemical element with symbol K (derived from Neo-Latin, kalium) and atomic number 19. It was first isolated from potash, the ashes of plants, from which its name is derived. In the Periodic table, potassium is one of seven elements in column (group) 1 (alkali metals):they all have a single valence electron in their outer electron shell, which they readily give up to create an atom with a positive charge - a cation, and combine with anions to form salts.
discoveryCountry: United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland

name: Calcium

image.title: Calcium Grains, grain size about 1 mm
image.attribution: Jurii, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, source: https://images-of-elements.com/calcium.php

funFacts: Calcium is the fifth most abundant element in Earth's crust and the most abundant metal in the human body.
Your bones and teeth are mostly made of calcium phosphate — an adult skeleton contains about 1 kg of calcium.
Chalk, limestone, marble, and pearls are all different forms of calcium carbonate (CaCO₃).
summary: Calcium is a chemical element with symbol Ca and atomic number 20. Calcium is a soft gray alkaline earth metal, fifth-most-abundant element by mass in the Earth's crust. The ion Ca2+ is also the fifth-most-abundant dissolved ion in seawater by both molarity and mass, after sodium, chloride, magnesium, and sulfate.
discoveryCountry: United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland

name: Scandium

image.title: Crystal of Scandium. About 1g
image.attribution: JanDerChemiker, CC BY-SA 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

funFacts: Scandium is so rare that until recently, the entire global supply was only a few kilograms per year.
Adding a small amount of scandium to aluminum alloys dramatically increases their strength — used in Russian MiG fighter jets.
Scandium was the first element predicted by Mendeleev that was later discovered exactly as he described.
summary: Scandium is a chemical element with symbol Sc and atomic number 21. A silvery-white metallic d-block element, it has historically been sometimes classified as a rare earth element, together with yttrium and the lanthanoids. It was discovered in 1879 by spectral analysis of the minerals euxenite and gadolinite from Scandinavia.

name: Titanium

image.title: Titanium Crystal made with the van Arkel-de Booer Process. 87 grams, Original size in cm: 2.5 x 4
image.attribution: Hi-Res Images ofChemical Elements, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, source: https://images-of-elements.com/titanium.php

funFacts: Titanium is as strong as steel but 45% lighter, making it ideal for aircraft, spacecraft, and medical implants.
Titanium is completely biocompatible — the human body does not reject it, so it's used in hip replacements and dental implants.
Titanium dioxide (TiO₂) is the white pigment in most white paints, sunscreens, and white food coloring.
summary: Titanium is a chemical element with symbol Ti and atomic number 22. It is a lustrous transition metal with a silver color, low density and high strength. It is highly resistant to corrosion in sea water, aqua regia and chlorine.

name: Vanadium

image.title: Pieces of Pure Vanadium with Oxide Layer
image.attribution: Jurii, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, source: https://images-of-elements.com/vanadium.php

funFacts: Vanadium steel, with just a small percentage of vanadium, is used in car chassis and tools for its extreme toughness.
Sea cucumbers and certain marine worms use vanadium instead of iron in their blood.
Vanadium flow batteries are a promising technology for large-scale grid energy storage.
summary: Vanadium is a chemical element with symbol V and atomic number 23. It is a hard, silvery grey, ductile and malleable transition metal. The element is found only in chemically combined form in nature, but once isolated artificially, the formation of an oxide layer stabilizes the free metal somewhat against further oxidation.

name: Chromium

image.title: Piece of Chromium Metal
image.attribution: Jurii, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, source: https://images-of-elements.com/chromium.php

funFacts: Chromium gives rubies their red color and emeralds their green — the same element produces opposite colors in different minerals.
Stainless steel is iron with at least 10.5% chromium — the chromium forms an invisible oxide layer that prevents rust.
Chrome plating uses electroplating to deposit a thin, shiny layer of chromium for both appearance and corrosion resistance.
summary: Chromium is a chemical element with symbol Cr and atomic number 24. It is the first element in Group 6. It is a steely-gray, lustrous, hard and brittle metal which takes a high polish, resists tarnishing, and has a high melting point.

name: Manganese

image.title: Two Oieces of Manganese Metal
image.attribution: W. Oelen, CC BY-SA 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

funFacts: Manganese nodules on the ocean floor contain vast amounts of manganese, iron, nickel, and cobalt — a future mining target.
Steel without manganese would be so brittle it would shatter rather than bend — manganese is essential in steelmaking.
The purple color of amethyst crystals comes from traces of manganese and iron impurities.
summary: Manganese is a chemical element with symbol Mn and atomic number 25. It is not found as a free element in nature; it is often found in combination with iron, and in many minerals. Manganese is a metal with important industrial metal alloy uses, particularly in stainless steels.

name: Iron
appearance: lustrous metallic with a grayish tinge

image.title: Fragments of an iron meteorite, about 92% iron. Original size of the single pieces in cm: 0.4 - 0.8
image.attribution: Chemical ELements A Virtual Museum, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0> source: https://images-of-elements.com/iron.php

funFacts: Iron is the most abundant element on Earth by mass — Earth's core is mostly iron and nickel.
The red color of Mars comes from iron oxide (rust) covering its surface.
Iron is the heaviest element that can be formed by nuclear fusion in stars — heavier elements require supernova explosions.
summary: Iron is a chemical element with symbol Fe (from Latin:ferrum) and atomic number 26. It is a metal in the first transition series. It is by mass the most common element on Earth, forming much of Earth's outer and inner core.

name: Cobalt

image.title: Fractions from a cobalt, 7 and 4 grams. Original size in cm: 2 x 2
image.attribution: Hi-Res Images ofChemical Elements, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, source: https://images-of-elements.com/cobalt.php

funFacts: Cobalt-60 is used in cancer radiation therapy to destroy tumors with gamma rays.
The brilliant blue in medieval stained glass windows comes from cobalt compounds — it's been used as a pigment for millennia.
Vitamin B12, essential for human health, is the only vitamin that contains a metal — cobalt at its center.
summary: Cobalt is a chemical element with symbol Co and atomic number 27. Like nickel, cobalt in the Earth's crust is found only in chemically combined form, save for small deposits found in alloys of natural meteoric iron. The free element, produced by reductive smelting, is a hard, lustrous, silver-gray metal.

name: Nickel
appearance: lustrous, metallic, and silver with a gold tinge

image.title: Nickel Chunk
image.attribution: Materialscientist at English Wikipedia, CC BY-SA 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

funFacts: The nickel coin in the US is actually 75% copper and only 25% nickel — despite being named after the metal.
Nickel is used in rechargeable NiMH batteries found in hybrid cars and many consumer electronics.
Stainless steel cutlery typically contains about 8–10% nickel, which adds corrosion resistance and shine.
summary: Nickel is a chemical element with symbol Ni and atomic number 28. It is a silvery-white lustrous metal with a slight golden tinge. Nickel belongs to the transition metals and is hard and ductile.

name: Copper

image.title: Macro of Native Copper about 1 ½ inches (4 cm) in size
image.attribution: Native_Copper_Macro_Digon3.jpg: 'Jonathan Zander (Digon3)' derivative work: Materialscientist, CC BY-SA 2.5 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/2.5>, via Wikimedia Commons

funFacts: Copper was the first metal to be worked by humans, over 10,000 years ago — the Copper Age preceded the Bronze Age.
Copper has natural antimicrobial properties — bacteria die when they touch copper surfaces, which is why hospitals are using it more.
The Statue of Liberty is made of copper and gets its green color (patina) from weathering — it was originally shiny orange-brown.
summary: Copper is a chemical element with symbol Cu (from Latin:cuprum) and atomic number 29. It is a soft, malleable and ductile metal with very high thermal and electrical conductivity. A freshly exposed surface of pure copper has a reddish-orange color.

name: Zinc

image.title: 30 grams Zinc, front and back side. Original size in cm: 3
image.attribution: Hi-Res Images ofChemical Elements, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, source: https://images-of-elements.com/zinc.php

funFacts: Zinc is essential for immune function — a zinc deficiency is one of the most common nutritional deficiencies worldwide.
Galvanizing steel with a thin layer of zinc prevents rust and is why lamp posts, fences, and guardrails last for decades.
Pennies minted after 1982 in the US are 97.5% zinc with just a thin copper coating.
summary: Zinc, in commerce also spelter, is a chemical element with symbol Zn and atomic number 30. It is the first element of group 12 of the periodic table. In some respects zinc is chemically similar to magnesium:its ion is of similar size and its only common oxidation state is +2.

name: Gallium

image.title: Solid gallium, fresh and after some time (2 months) at room temperature
image.attribution: Hi-Res Images ofChemical Elements, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, source: https://images-of-elements.com/gallium.php

funFacts: Gallium melts at just 29.8°C (85.6°F) — it will melt in the palm of your hand.
Gallium arsenide (GaAs) is used in solar cells for satellites and spacecraft because it's more efficient than silicon.
LEDs and laser diodes in Blu-ray players rely on gallium nitride semiconductors.
summary: Gallium is a chemical element with symbol Ga and atomic number 31. Elemental gallium does not occur in free form in nature, but as the gallium(III) compounds that are in trace amounts in zinc ores and in bauxite. Gallium is a soft, silvery metal, and elemental gallium is a brittle solid at low temperatures, and melts at 29.76 °C (85.57 °F) (slightly above room temperature).

name: Germanium

image.title: 12 Grams Polycrystalline Germanium, 2*3 cm
image.attribution: Jurii, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, source: https://images-of-elements.com/germanium.php

funFacts: Germanium was predicted by Mendeleev as 'eka-silicon' in 1871 before it was discovered in 1886.
The first working transistors were made from germanium before silicon took over in the 1960s.
Germanium lenses are used in infrared cameras and night-vision equipment because it's transparent to infrared light.
summary: Germanium is a chemical element with symbol Ge and atomic number 32. It is a lustrous, hard, grayish-white metalloid in the carbon group, chemically similar to its group neighbors tin and silicon. Purified germanium is a semiconductor, with an appearance most similar to elemental silicon.

name: Arsenic

image.title: Ultrapure Metallic Arsenic under Argon, 1 - 2 grams. Original size of each piece in cm: 0.5 x 1
image.attribution: Hi-Res Images ofChemical Elements, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, source: https://images-of-elements.com/arsenic.php

funFacts: Arsenic was the poison of choice for murderers throughout history — it was called 'inheritance powder' in the Middle Ages.
Arsenic compounds were once used as green dyes in Victorian wallpaper, causing mysterious illnesses in damp homes.
Arsenic-based drugs were the first chemotherapy agents — Paul Ehrlich used arsenic compounds to treat syphilis in 1910.
summary: Arsenic is a chemical element with symbol As and atomic number 33. Arsenic occurs in many minerals, usually in conjunction with sulfur and metals, and also as a pure elemental crystal. Arsenic is a metalloid.

name: Selenium
appearance: black, red, and gray (not pictured) allotropes

image.title: Ultrapure Black, Amorphous Selenium, 3 - 4 grams. Original size in cm: 2
image.attribution: Hi-Res Images ofChemical Elements, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, source: https://images-of-elements.com/selenium.php

funFacts: Selenium is essential for human health in tiny amounts but toxic at higher doses — the difference is very small.
The first photocopiers (Xerox machines) used selenium drums to transfer toner to paper through static electricity.
Brazil nuts are the richest food source of selenium — eating too many can cause selenium toxicity.
summary: Selenium is a chemical element with symbol Se and atomic number 34. It is a nonmetal with properties that are intermediate between those of its periodic table column-adjacent chalcogen elements sulfur and tellurium. It rarely occurs in its elemental state in nature, or as pure ore compounds.

name: Bromine

image.title: 99.5 % pure liquid Bromine in a 4 x 1 cm big glass ampoule, cast in acrylic
image.attribution: Jurii, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, source: https://images-of-elements.com/bromine.php

funFacts: Bromine is one of only two elements that are liquid at room temperature (the other is mercury).
Ancient Phoenicians extracted a purple dye called Tyrian purple from sea snails — it contains a bromine compound.
Bromide compounds were used as sedatives and anticonvulsants in the 19th century — 'bromide' still means a boring platitude.
summary: Bromine (from Ancient Greek:βρῶμος, brómos, meaning "stench") is a chemical element with symbol Br, and atomic number 35. It is a halogen. The element was isolated independently by two chemists, Carl Jacob Löwig and Antoine Jerome Balard, in 1825–1826.

name: Krypton
appearance: colorless gas, exhibiting a whitish glow in a high electric field

image.title: Vial of Glowing Ultrapure Krypton. Original size in cm: 1 x 5.
image.attribution: Jurii, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, source: https://images-of-elements.com/krypton.php

funFacts: Krypton is used to calibrate scientific instruments — the krypton-86 spectral line was once the official definition of the meter.
Krypton fluoride lasers are used in semiconductor manufacturing to create the tiny circuits in microchips.
Despite its fictional association with Superman's weakness, real krypton is completely harmless and inert.
summary: Krypton (from Greek:κρυπτός kryptos "the hidden one") is a chemical element with symbol Kr and atomic number 36. It is a member of group 18 (noble gases) elements. A colorless, odorless, tasteless noble gas, krypton occurs in trace amounts in the atmosphere, is isolated by fractionally distilling liquefied air, and is often used with other rare gases in fluorescent lamps.

name: Rubidium

image.title: Rubidium Metal Sample
image.attribution: Dnn87, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

funFacts: Rubidium atomic clocks are the most common type of atomic clock, found in GPS satellites and cell towers.
Rubidium is so reactive that it must be stored in dry mineral oil or a sealed inert atmosphere.
A rubidium Bose-Einstein condensate — matter at near absolute zero — was one of the first ever created in 1995.
summary: Rubidium is a chemical element with symbol Rb and atomic number 37. Rubidium is a soft, silvery-white metallic element of the alkali metal group, with an atomic mass of 85.4678. Elemental rubidium is highly reactive, with properties similar to those of other alkali metals, such as very rapid oxidation in air.

name: Strontium

image.title: Strontium Pieces under Paraffin Oil.
image.attribution: Jurii, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, source: https://images-of-elements.com/strontium.php

funFacts: Strontium compounds give fireworks their brilliant red color — strontium salts burn with an intense crimson flame.
Strontium-90, a radioactive isotope from nuclear fallout, is dangerous because the body mistakes it for calcium in bones.
Strontium ranelate was used as a medication to treat osteoporosis by stimulating bone formation.
summary: Strontium is a chemical element with symbol Sr and atomic number 38. An alkaline earth metal, strontium is a soft silver-white or yellowish metallic element that is highly reactive chemically. The metal turns yellow when it is exposed to air.

name: Yttrium

image.title: 6,21g Yttrium, Reinheit mindestens 99%.
image.attribution: Jan Anskeit, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

funFacts: Yttrium is used in the red phosphor of old CRT television screens and monitors.
Yttrium-aluminum garnet (YAG) crystals are used in the most common type of solid-state laser.
Yttrium, ytterbium, terbium, and erbium are all named after the village of Ytterby, Sweden — where they were discovered.
summary: Yttrium is a chemical element with symbol Y and atomic number 39. It is a silvery-metallic transition metal chemically similar to the lanthanides and it has often been classified as a "rare earth element". Yttrium is almost always found combined with the lanthanides in rare earth minerals and is never found in nature as a free element.

name: Zirconium

image.title: Two pieces of Zirconium, 1 cm each.
image.attribution: Jurii, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, source: https://images-of-elements.com/zirconium.php

funFacts: Zirconium is highly resistant to corrosion and is used in nuclear reactor fuel rods because neutrons barely affect it.
Cubic zirconia (ZrO₂), a synthetic gemstone, is the most popular diamond simulant — it's optically flawless and cheap.
Zirconium silicate crystals have been found that are 4.4 billion years old — the oldest known material on Earth.
summary: Zirconium is a chemical element with symbol Zr and atomic number 40. The name of zirconium is taken from the name of the mineral zircon, the most important source of zirconium. The word zircon comes from the Persian word zargun زرگون, meaning "gold-colored".

name: Niobium

image.title: Niobium strips
image.attribution: Mauro Cateb, CC BY-SA 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

funFacts: Niobium is added to steel in tiny amounts to dramatically increase its strength — most modern high-strength steel contains it.
Niobium becomes a superconductor at -264°C and is used in MRI machine magnets.
Brazil holds about 98% of the world's known niobium reserves, making it strategically critical.
summary: Niobium, formerly columbium, is a chemical element with symbol Nb (formerly Cb) and atomic number 41. It is a soft, grey, ductile transition metal, which is often found in the pyrochlore mineral, the main commercial source for niobium, and columbite. The name comes from Greek mythology:Niobe, daughter of Tantalus since it is so similar to tantalum.

name: Molybdenum

image.title: 99.9 Pure Molybdenum Crystal, about 2 x 3 cm, with anodisation color
image.attribution: Jurii, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, source: https://images-of-elements.com/molybdenum.php

funFacts: Molybdenum has the sixth highest melting point of any element — it doesn't melt until 2,623°C.
Molybdenum is essential for nitrogen fixation in plants — the enzyme nitrogenase needs it to convert atmospheric nitrogen.
Molybdenum disulfide is a dry lubricant used in extreme conditions where oil would burn away.
summary: Molybdenum is a chemical element with symbol Mo and atomic number 42. The name is from Neo-Latin molybdaenum, from Ancient Greek Μόλυβδος molybdos, meaning lead, since its ores were confused with lead ores. Molybdenum minerals have been known throughout history, but the element was discovered (in the sense of differentiating it as a new entity from the mineral salts of other metals) in 1778 by Carl Wilhelm Scheele.

name: Technetium

image.title: Technetium Sample inside a sealed glass ampoule, filled with argon gas. 6x1 mm goldfoil covered with 99Tc powder (electroplated).
image.attribution: GFDL, CC BY-SA 4.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

funFacts: Technetium was the first element to be artificially produced — it doesn't exist naturally on Earth in significant amounts.
Technetium-99m is the most widely used radioisotope in nuclear medicine, used in over 40 million medical scans per year.
Despite being radioactive, technetium-99m has a half-life of only 6 hours, so it clears from the body quickly.
summary: Technetium (/tɛkˈniːʃiəm/) is a chemical element with symbol Tc and atomic number 43. It is the element with the lowest atomic number in the periodic table that has no stable isotopes:every form of it is radioactive. Nearly all technetium is produced synthetically, and only minute amounts are found in nature.

name: Ruthenium

image.title: Ruthenium Crystal, 0.6 grams, 0.6 x 1.3 cm size
image.attribution: Hi-Res Images ofChemical Elements, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, source: https://images-of-elements.com/ruthenium.php

funFacts: Ruthenium is one of the rarest elements on Earth but adds extreme hardness to platinum alloys used in pen nibs.
Ruthenium-based catalysts are crucial for ammonia synthesis in the Haber process, which feeds half the world.
Ruthenium red, a biological stain, is used in electron microscopy to highlight cell membranes.
summary: Ruthenium is a chemical element with symbol Ru and atomic number 44. It is a rare transition metal belonging to the platinum group of the periodic table. Like the other metals of the platinum group, ruthenium is inert to most other chemicals.

name: Rhodium

image.title: Pure Rhodium Bead, 1 gram. Original size in cm: 0.5
image.attribution: Hi-Res Images ofChemical Elements, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, source: https://images-of-elements.com/rhodium.php

funFacts: Rhodium is the most expensive precious metal — it has been worth over 10 times the price of gold.
The catalytic converter in your car uses rhodium, platinum, and palladium to convert toxic exhaust gases into harmless ones.
Rhodium's extreme reflectivity makes it ideal for reflective coatings on mirrors, searchlights, and jewelry.
summary: Rhodium is a chemical element with symbol Rh and atomic number 45. It is a rare, silvery-white, hard, and chemically inert transition metal. It is a member of the platinum group.
discoveryCountry: United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland

name: Palladium

image.title: Palladium Crystal, about 1 gram. Original size in cm: 0.5 x 1
image.attribution: Hi-Res Images ofChemical Elements, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, source: https://images-of-elements.com/palladium.php

funFacts: Palladium can absorb up to 900 times its own volume of hydrogen gas, making it useful for hydrogen storage.
Palladium was discovered in 1803 by William Hyde Wollaston, who secretly sold it for two years before announcing it.
White gold jewelry is often gold alloyed with palladium — it's cheaper and lighter than platinum white gold.
summary: Palladium is a chemical element with symbol Pd and atomic number 46. It is a rare and lustrous silvery-white metal discovered in 1803 by William Hyde Wollaston. He named it after the asteroid Pallas, which was itself named after the epithet of the Greek goddess Athena, acquired by her when she slew Pallas.
discoveryCountry: United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland

name: Silver

image.title: Natural silver nugget, 1 cm long.
image.attribution: Jurii, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, source: http://images-of-elements.com/silver.php

funFacts: Silver has the highest electrical conductivity of any element — even higher than copper.
Silver nanoparticles are used in antibacterial coatings on medical devices, bandages, and even socks.
The expression 'born with a silver spoon' comes from the fact that silver tableware actually has antimicrobial benefits.
summary: Silver is a chemical element with symbol Ag (Greek:άργυρος árguros, Latin:argentum, both from the Indo-European root *h₂erǵ- for "grey" or "shining") and atomic number 47. A soft, white, lustrous transition metal, it possesses the highest electrical conductivity, thermal conductivity and reflectivity of any metal. The metal occurs naturally in its pure, free form (native silver), as an alloy with gold and other metals, and in minerals such as argentite and chlorargyrite.

name: Cadmium

image.title: 48 Cd Cadmium
image.attribution: Chemical Elements A Virtual Museum under a Creative Commons Attribution 3.0 Unported License, source: https://images-of-elements.com/cadmium.php

funFacts: Cadmium is highly toxic and accumulates in the kidneys — 'Itai-itai disease' in Japan was caused by cadmium-contaminated rice.
NiCd rechargeable batteries, once common in electronics, use cadmium but are being phased out due to toxicity.
Cadmium sulfide is a brilliant yellow pigment used by artists — it was a favorite of Henri Matisse.
summary: Cadmium is a chemical element with symbol Cd and atomic number 48. This soft, bluish-white metal is chemically similar to the two other stable metals in group 12, zinc and mercury. Like zinc, it prefers oxidation state +2 in most of its compounds and like mercury it shows a low melting point compared to transition metals.

name: Indium

image.title: 1.5 x 1.5 cm liquid indium
image.attribution: Jurii, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, source: http://images-of-elements.com/indium.php

funFacts: Indium tin oxide (ITO) is the transparent conductor used on every touchscreen and LCD display in the world.
Indium is so soft it can be scratched with a fingernail and squeaks when bent — a phenomenon called 'tin cry.'
Indium is primarily obtained as a byproduct of zinc smelting — without zinc production, there would be very little indium.
summary: Indium is a chemical element with symbol In and atomic number 49. It is a post-transition metallic element that is rare in Earth's crust. The metal is very soft, malleable and easily fusible, with a melting point higher than sodium, but lower than lithium or tin.

name: Tin

image.title: Tin blob
image.attribution: Jurii, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, source: http://images-of-elements.com/tin.php

funFacts: Tin whiskers — tiny metallic filaments that spontaneously grow on tin surfaces — have caused failures in spacecraft and pacemakers.
Bronze, the alloy that defined the Bronze Age, is 88% copper and 12% tin — it was harder than either metal alone.
Tin cans are actually made mostly of steel with only a thin coating of tin to prevent corrosion.
summary: Tin is a chemical element with the symbol Sn (for Latin:stannum) and atomic number 50. It is a main group metal in group 14 of the periodic table. Tin shows a chemical similarity to both neighboring group-14 elements, germanium and lead, and has two possible oxidation states, +2 and the slightly more stable +4.

name: Antimony

image.title: Antimony crystal, 2 grams, 1 cm
image.attribution: Unknown authorUnknown author, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, source: https://images-of-elements.com/antimony.php

funFacts: Antimony sulfide (kohl) was used as eye makeup by ancient Egyptians — it also had antimicrobial properties.
Ancient Roman goblets made of antimony were used as 'purgative cups' — wine stored in them became an emetic.
Antimony trioxide is one of the most widely used flame retardants, added to plastics, textiles, and electronics.
summary: Antimony is a chemical element with symbol Sb (from Latin:stibium) and atomic number 51. A lustrous gray metalloid, it is found in nature mainly as the sulfide mineral stibnite (Sb2S3). Antimony compounds have been known since ancient times and were used for cosmetics; metallic antimony was also known, but it was erroneously identified as lead upon its discovery.

name: Tellurium

image.title: Metallic tellurium, diameter 3.5 cm
image.attribution: Unknown authorUnknown author, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, source: https://images-of-elements.com/tellurium.php

funFacts: Tellurium gives garlic its distinctive smell when ingested — even tiny amounts make breath smell of garlic for days.
Cadmium telluride (CdTe) solar panels are the second most common type in the world, after silicon.
Tellurium is one of the rarest stable elements on Earth — it's actually more rare than platinum.
summary: Tellurium is a chemical element with symbol Te and atomic number 52. It is a brittle, mildly toxic, rare, silver-white metalloid. Tellurium is chemically related to selenium and sulfur.

name: Iodine
appearance: lustrous metallic gray, violet as a gas

image.title: Iodine Sample
image.attribution: Benjah-bmm27, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

funFacts: Iodine deficiency is the leading preventable cause of intellectual disability worldwide — that's why table salt is iodized.
Iodine vapor is a brilliant purple color — its name comes from the Greek word 'ioeides' meaning violet-colored.
Povidone-iodine (Betadine), the brown antiseptic, kills bacteria, viruses, and fungi on contact.
summary: Iodine is a chemical element with symbol I and atomic number 53. The name is from Greek ἰοειδής ioeidēs, meaning violet or purple, due to the color of iodine vapor. Iodine and its compounds are primarily used in nutrition, and industrially in the production of acetic acid and certain polymers.

name: Xenon
appearance: colorless gas, exhibiting a blue glow when placed in a high voltage electric field

image.title: Vial of glowing ultrapure xenon. Original size in cm: 1 x 5
image.attribution: Jurii, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, source: https://images-of-elements.com/xenon.php

funFacts: Xenon anesthesia was used in surgery — patients wake up faster and with fewer side effects than other anesthetics.
Xenon ion thrusters power many deep-space spacecraft — ionized xenon atoms are accelerated to provide thrust.
Xenon was the first noble gas found to form chemical compounds, discovered by Neil Bartlett in 1962 — shocking chemists worldwide.
summary: Xenon is a chemical element with symbol Xe and atomic number 54. It is a colorless, dense, odorless noble gas, that occurs in the Earth's atmosphere in trace amounts. Although generally unreactive, xenon can undergo a few chemical reactions such as the formation of xenon hexafluoroplatinate, the first noble gas compound to be synthesized.

name: Cesium

image.title: Cesium/Caesium metal
image.attribution: Dnn87 Contact email: Dnn87yahoo.dk, CC BY-SA 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

funFacts: Cesium atomic clocks are so accurate that they would only lose one second in 300 million years — they define the second.
Cesium is the most electropositive stable element and reacts explosively even with ice.
GPS satellites rely on cesium atomic clocks to maintain the precise timing needed for location accuracy.
summary: Caesium or cesium is a chemical element with symbol Cs and atomic number 55. It is a soft, silvery-gold alkali metal with a melting point of 28 °C (82 °F), which makes it one of only five elemental metals that are liquid at or near room temperature. Caesium is an alkali metal and has physical and chemical properties similar to those of rubidium and potassium.

name: Barium

image.title: 1.5 Grams Barium with a Grey Oxide Layer under Argon. Original size in cm: 0.7 x 1
image.attribution: Hi-Res Images ofChemical Elements, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, source: https://images-of-elements.com/barium.php

funFacts: Barium sulfate is completely insoluble and non-toxic — patients drink it as a 'barium meal' to make their digestive tract visible in X-rays.
Barium compounds create the green color in fireworks.
Despite the name, barite (barium sulfate) is used in oil drilling as a weighting agent in drilling fluid.
summary: Barium is a chemical element with symbol Ba and atomic number 56. It is the fifth element in Group 2, a soft silvery metallic alkaline earth metal. Because of its high chemical reactivity barium is never found in nature as a free element.

name: Lanthanum

image.title: 1 cm Big Piece of Pure Lanthanum
image.attribution: Jurii, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, source: https://images-of-elements.com/lanthanum.php

funFacts: Lanthanum is used in high-end camera and telescope lenses to reduce chromatic aberration.
Nickel-metal hydride batteries in hybrid cars use a lanthanum-rich alloy to store hydrogen.
Lanthanum gives certain types of studio lighting glass a distinctive yellow-green tint.
summary: Lanthanum is a soft, ductile, silvery-white metallic chemical element with symbol La and atomic number 57. It tarnishes rapidly when exposed to air and is soft enough to be cut with a knife. It gave its name to the lanthanide series, a group of 15 similar elements between lanthanum and lutetium in the periodic table:it is also sometimes considered the first element of the 6th-period transition metals.

name: Cerium

image.title: Ultrapure Cerium under Argon, 1.5 grams. Original size in cm: 1 x 1
image.attribution: Jurii, CC BY 1.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/1.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, source: https://images-of-elements.com/cerium.php

funFacts: Cerium is the most abundant rare earth element — despite being called 'rare,' it's more common than copper.
Cerium oxide is used to polish glass and as a catalytic converter coating that removes toxic emissions.
Flint lighters contain a 'misch metal' alloy rich in cerium — it produces sparks when struck.
summary: Cerium is a chemical element with symbol Ce and atomic number 58. It is a soft, silvery, ductile metal which easily oxidizes in air. Cerium was named after the dwarf planet Ceres (itself named after the Roman goddess of agriculture).

name: Praseodymium

image.title: 1.5 Grams Praseodymium under Argon, 0.5 cm big pieces
image.attribution: Jurii, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, source: https://images-of-elements.com/praseodymium.php

funFacts: Praseodymium gives the glass in welder's goggles its yellow-green tint, protecting eyes from bright glare.
Neodymium magnets are actually an alloy of neodymium, iron, and boron — and praseodymium is often substituted for neodymium.
Its name comes from the Greek for 'green twin' because it and neodymium were originally thought to be one element.
summary: Praseodymium is a chemical element with symbol Pr and atomic number 59. Praseodymium is a soft, silvery, malleable and ductile metal in the lanthanide group. It is valued for its magnetic, electrical, chemical, and optical properties.

name: Neodymium

image.title: Ultrapure Neodymium under Argon, 5 grams. Original length of the large piece in cm: 1
image.attribution: Hi-Res Images ofChemical Elements, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, source: https://images-of-elements.com/neodymium.php

funFacts: Neodymium magnets are the strongest type of permanent magnet and are found in every electric motor, hard drive, and headphone.
Neodymium glass lasers are used in inertial confinement fusion experiments — among the most powerful lasers ever built.
Neodymium gives special didymium glass its color, used in glassblowing to filter out the harsh yellow sodium flame.
summary: Neodymium is a chemical element with symbol Nd and atomic number 60. It is a soft silvery metal that tarnishes in air. Neodymium was discovered in 1885 by the Austrian chemist Carl Auer von Welsbach.

name: Promethium

image.title: Photomontage of what promethium metal might look like (it is too radioactive and real images are not available)
image.attribution: Unknown authorUnknown author, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, source: https://images-of-elements.com/promethium.php

funFacts: Promethium is the only lanthanide with no stable isotopes — all of it on Earth is artificially produced.
Promethium was used in early nuclear-powered pacemakers and luminous watch dials before better alternatives emerged.
It was named after Prometheus, the Titan who stole fire from the gods — because it was created in nuclear reactors.
summary: Promethium, originally prometheum, is a chemical element with the symbol Pm and atomic number 61. All of its isotopes are radioactive; it is one of only two such elements that are followed in the periodic table by elements with stable forms, a distinction shared with technetium. Chemically, promethium is a lanthanide, which forms salts when combined with other elements.

name: Samarium

image.title: Ultrapure Sublimated Samarium, 2 grams. Original size in cm: 0.8 x 1.5
image.attribution: Unknown authorUnknown author, CC BY 1.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/1.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, source: https://images-of-elements.com/samarium.php

funFacts: Samarium-cobalt magnets were the first rare earth magnets, used in applications requiring magnetism at high temperatures.
Samarium-153 is used in cancer pain treatment — it targets bone metastases and delivers localized radiation therapy.
Samarium was discovered in the mineral samarskite, which was named after a Russian mining official — Vasili Samarsky.
summary: Samarium is a chemical element with symbol Sm and atomic number 62. It is a moderately hard silvery metal that readily oxidizes in air. Being a typical member of the lanthanide series, samarium usually assumes the oxidation state +3.

name: Europium

image.title: Weakly Oxidized Europium, hence slightly yellowish. 1.5 grams, large piece 0.6 x 1.6 cm.
image.attribution: Jurii, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, source: https://images-of-elements.com/europium.php

funFacts: Europium makes the red and blue phosphors in old CRT televisions — without it, color TV wouldn't look right.
Euro banknotes use europium-based fluorescent ink as an anti-counterfeiting measure.
Europium is the most reactive rare earth element and oxidizes rapidly in air.
summary: Europium is a chemical element with symbol Eu and atomic number 63. It was isolated in 1901 and is named after the continent of Europe. It is a moderately hard, silvery metal which readily oxidizes in air and water.

name: Gadolinium

image.title: Pure (99.95%) Amorphous Gadolinium, about 12 grams, 2 × 1.5 × 0.5 cm, cast in acrylic glass
image.attribution: Jurii, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, source: https://images-of-elements.com/gadolinium.php

funFacts: Gadolinium-based contrast agents are injected before MRI scans — gadolinium's magnetic properties enhance image quality.
Gadolinium has the highest neutron absorption cross-section of any stable element, used in nuclear reactor control rods.
Gadolinium gallium garnet is used as a substrate for magnetic bubble memory and as a gemstone simulant.
summary: Gadolinium is a chemical element with symbol Gd and atomic number 64. It is a silvery-white, malleable and ductile rare-earth metal. It is found in nature only in combined (salt) form.

name: Terbium

image.title: Pure Terbium, 3 grams. Original size: 1 cm
image.attribution: Unknown authorUnknown author, CC BY 1.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/1.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, source: https://images-of-elements.com/terbium.php

funFacts: Terbium is used in green phosphors for LED lights and the green component of compact fluorescent bulbs.
Terfenol-D, a terbium alloy, changes shape in a magnetic field — used in sonar transducers and precision actuators.
Terbium and three other elements (ytterbium, erbium, yttrium) are all named after the same Swedish village, Ytterby.
summary: Terbium is a chemical element with symbol Tb and atomic number 65. It is a silvery-white rare earth metal that is malleable, ductile and soft enough to be cut with a knife. Terbium is never found in nature as a free element, but it is contained in many minerals, including cerite, gadolinite, monazite, xenotime and euxenite.

name: Dysprosium

image.title: Pure Dysprosium Dendrites
image.attribution: Jurii, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, source: https://images-of-elements.com/dysprosium.php

funFacts: Dysprosium is critical for making neodymium magnets work at high temperatures — adding it allows motors in electric cars to handle engine heat.
Dysprosium has the highest magnetic moment of any element, making it extremely useful in magnetic refrigeration research.
Its name means 'hard to get' in Greek — it took many separation attempts before pure dysprosium was isolated.
summary: Dysprosium is a chemical element with the symbol Dy and atomic number 66. It is a rare earth element with a metallic silver luster. Dysprosium is never found in nature as a free element, though it is found in various minerals, such as xenotime.

name: Holmium

image.title: Ultrapure Holmium, 17 grams. Original size in cm: 1.5 x 2.5
image.attribution: Unknown authorUnknown author, CC BY 1.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/1.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, source: https://images-of-elements.com/holmium.php

funFacts: Holmium has the highest magnetic moment of any naturally occurring element.
Holmium lasers (Ho:YAG) are used in urology to break up kidney stones without damaging surrounding tissue.
Holmium oxide gives a distinctive yellow color to glass and is used to calibrate spectrophotometers.
summary: Holmium is a chemical element with symbol Ho and atomic number 67. Part of the lanthanide series, holmium is a rare earth element. Holmium was discovered by Swedish chemist Per Theodor Cleve.

name: Erbium

image.title: 9.5 Gramms Pure Erbium, 2 x 2 cm
image.attribution: Jurii, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, source: https://images-of-elements.com/erbium.php

funFacts: Erbium-doped fiber amplifiers (EDFAs) are the technology behind long-distance internet cables — they boost optical signals without converting to electricity.
Erbium lasers are used in dermatology for skin resurfacing because water absorbs their wavelength extremely well.
Erbium oxide gives glass and porcelain a distinctive pink color, used in decorative applications.
summary: Erbium is a chemical element in the lanthanide series, with symbol Er and atomic number 68. A silvery-white solid metal when artificially isolated, natural erbium is always found in chemical combination with other elements on Earth. As such, it is a rare earth element which is associated with several other rare elements in the mineral gadolinite from Ytterby in Sweden, where yttrium, ytterbium, and terbium were discovered.

name: Thulium

image.title: Ultrapure (99.997%) Crystalline Thulium, 22.3 grams, 3 × 3 × 2 cm in size
image.attribution: Jurii, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, source: https://images-of-elements.com/thulium.php

funFacts: Thulium is one of the rarest and least-known lanthanides — global production is only a few hundred kilograms per year.
Thulium-170 is used in portable X-ray devices for medical and industrial use in remote locations without power.
Thulium lasers have potential applications in ophthalmology for eye surgery.
summary: Thulium is a chemical element with symbol Tm and atomic number 69. It is the thirteenth and antepenultimate (third-last) element in the lanthanide series. Like the other lanthanides, the most common oxidation state is +3, seen in its oxide, halides and other compounds.

name: Ytterbium

image.title: Ytterbium, 0.5 x 1 cm
image.attribution: Jurii, CC BY 1.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/1.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, source: https://images-of-elements.com/ytterbium.php

funFacts: Ytterbium atomic clocks are the most accurate timekeepers ever made — they can measure time to within 1.4 × 10⁻¹⁸ seconds.
Ytterbium lasers are used in materials processing, eye surgery, and as efficient pump sources for other lasers.
Ytterbium was discovered in the mineral gadolinite from Ytterby, Sweden — the fourth element named after that village.
summary: Ytterbium is a chemical element with symbol Yb and atomic number 70. It is the fourteenth and penultimate element in the lanthanide series, which is the basis of the relative stability of its +2 oxidation state. However, like the other lanthanides, its most common oxidation state is +3, seen in its oxide, halides and other compounds.

name: Lutetium

image.title: 1 cm Big Piece of Pure Lutetium
image.attribution: Jurii, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, source: https://images-of-elements.com/lutetium.php

funFacts: Lutetium is the densest and hardest of all lanthanides.
Lutetium oxyorthosilicate (LSO) scintillator crystals are used in PET scanners for cancer detection.
Lutetium-177 is a promising radiopharmaceutical for treating neuroendocrine tumors and prostate cancer.
summary: Lutetium is a chemical element with symbol Lu and atomic number 71. It is a silvery white metal, which resists corrosion in dry, but not in moist air. It is considered the first element of the 6th-period transition metals and the last element in the lanthanide series, and is traditionally counted among the rare earths.

name: Hafnium

image.title: Electrolytic Hafnium, 22 grams. Original size in cm: 1 x 2 x 3
image.attribution: Hi-Res Images ofChemical Elements, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, source: https://images-of-elements.com/hafnium.php

funFacts: Hafnium is always found alongside zirconium in nature and is chemically so similar that separating them is extremely difficult.
Hafnium is used in nuclear reactor control rods because it absorbs neutrons exceptionally well.
Intel uses hafnium oxide in modern transistor gate insulators — it was key to continuing Moore's Law after silicon limits.
summary: Hafnium is a chemical element with symbol Hf and atomic number 72. A lustrous, silvery gray, tetravalent transition metal, hafnium chemically resembles zirconium and is found in zirconium minerals. Its existence was predicted by Dmitri Mendeleev in 1869, though it was not identified until 1923, making it the penultimate stable element to be discovered (rhenium was identified two years later).

name: Tantalum

image.title: Piece of tantalum, 1 cm in size
image.attribution: Jurii, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, source: https://images-of-elements.com/tantalum.php

funFacts: Tantalum capacitors are in virtually every smartphone, laptop, and portable electronic device.
Tantalum is biologically inert and used in surgical implants, pacemaker components, and bone repair.
Conflict over coltan (columbite-tantalite) mines in the Democratic Republic of Congo has fueled armed conflict.
summary: Tantalum is a chemical element with symbol Ta and atomic number 73. Previously known as tantalium, its name comes from Tantalus, an antihero from Greek mythology. Tantalum is a rare, hard, blue-gray, lustrous transition metal that is highly corrosion-resistant.

name: Tungsten

image.title: Tungsten rod with oxidised surface, 80 grams. Original size in cm: 1.3 x 3
image.attribution: Jurii, CC BY 1.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/1.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, source: https://images-of-elements.com/tungsten.php

funFacts: Tungsten has the highest melting point of any element — it doesn't melt until 3,422°C (6,192°F).
Incandescent light bulb filaments were made of tungsten for over 100 years because of its extreme heat resistance.
Tungsten carbide is second only to diamond in hardness — used in cutting tools, drill bits, and armor-piercing ammunition.
summary: Tungsten, also known as wolfram, is a chemical element with symbol W and atomic number 74. The word tungsten comes from the Swedish language tung sten, which directly translates to heavy stone. Its name in Swedish is volfram, however, in order to distinguish it from scheelite, which in Swedish is alternatively named tungsten.

name: Rhenium

image.title: Pure Rhenium Bead, arc melted, 21 grams. Original size in cm: 1.5 x 1.7. Measured radiation dose <0.05 μS/h.
image.attribution: Hi-Res Images ofChemical Elements, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, source: https://images-of-elements.com/rhenium.php

funFacts: Rhenium has the second highest melting point of any element and is one of the rarest elements in Earth's crust.
Rhenium is added to nickel superalloys in jet engine turbine blades to allow operation at extreme temperatures.
Rhenium was the last naturally occurring stable element to be discovered, in 1925.
summary: Rhenium is a chemical element with symbol Re and atomic number 75. It is a silvery-white, heavy, third-row transition metal in group 7 of the periodic table. With an estimated average concentration of 1 part per billion (ppb), rhenium is one of the rarest elements in the Earth's crust.

name: Osmium

image.title: Pure Osmium Bead
image.attribution: Jurii, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, source: https://images-of-elements.com/osmium.php

funFacts: Osmium is the densest naturally occurring element — a golf ball-sized chunk would weigh over 1.4 kg.
Osmium tetroxide has a distinctive smell — the element's name comes from the Greek word 'osme' meaning odor.
Osmium-iridium alloys are the hardest metal alloys known and were used in fountain pen nibs and compass bearings.
summary: Osmium (from Greek osme (ὀσμή) meaning "smell") is a chemical element with symbol Os and atomic number 76. It is a hard, brittle, bluish-white transition metal in the platinum group that is found as a trace element in alloys, mostly in platinum ores. Osmium is the densest naturally occurring element, with a density of 22.59 g/cm3.
discoveryCountry: United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland

name: Iridium

image.title: Pieces of Pure Iridium, 1 gram. Original size: 0.1 - 0.3 cm each
image.attribution: Unknown authorUnknown author, CC BY 1.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/1.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, source: https://images-of-elements.com/iridium.php

funFacts: The Cretaceous–Paleogene boundary (the layer marking the dinosaur extinction) has elevated iridium levels — from the asteroid impact.
Iridium is the most corrosion-resistant element — nothing dissolves it except certain molten salts at extreme temperatures.
The standard kilogram prototype used to be made of a platinum-iridium alloy to resist corrosion and wear.
summary: Iridium is a chemical element with symbol Ir and atomic number 77. A very hard, brittle, silvery-white transition metal of the platinum group, iridium is generally credited with being the second densest element (after osmium) based on measured density, although calculations involving the space lattices of the elements show that iridium is denser. It is also the most corrosion-resistant metal, even at temperatures as high as 2000 °C. Although only certain molten salts and halogens are corrosive to solid iridium, finely divided iridium dust is much more reactive and can be flammable.

name: Platinum

image.title: Crystals of Pure Platinum grown by gas phase transport
image.attribution: Periodictableru, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

funFacts: About 40% of all manufactured goods either contain platinum or use platinum in their production process.
Cisplatin, a platinum compound, is one of the most widely used chemotherapy drugs for treating cancer.
Platinum catalysts in catalytic converters have prevented billions of tons of toxic gases from entering the atmosphere.
summary: Platinum is a chemical element with symbol Pt and atomic number 78. It is a dense, malleable, ductile, highly unreactive, precious, gray-white transition metal. Its name is derived from the Spanish term platina, which is literally translated into "little silver".

name: Gold

image.title: Ultrapure Gold Leaf
image.attribution: Hi-Res Images ofChemical Elements, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, source: https://images-of-elements.com/gold.php

funFacts: All the gold ever mined in human history would fit in about 3.5 Olympic swimming pools.
Gold is so chemically inert that the gold in Egyptian pharaohs' tombs is still shiny after 3,000 years.
Gold nanoparticles appear red or purple, not gold-colored — at the nanoscale, the optical properties completely change.
summary: Gold is a chemical element with symbol Au (from Latin:aurum) and atomic number 79. In its purest form, it is a bright, slightly reddish yellow, dense, soft, malleable and ductile metal. Chemically, gold is a transition metal and a group 11 element.

name: Mercury

image.title: 6 grams pure mercury. Diameter of the inner disc: 2 cm
image.attribution: Hi-Res Images of Chemical Elements, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, source: https://images-of-elements.com/mercury.php

funFacts: Mercury is the only metal that is liquid at room temperature — its freezing point is -38.8°C.
Ancient Romans used mercury to extract gold and silver from ore — mercury amalgam mining poisoned countless workers.
The phrase 'mad as a hatter' comes from hat-makers who used mercury in felt processing and suffered neurological damage.
summary: Mercury is a chemical element with symbol Hg and atomic number 80. It is commonly known as quicksilver and was formerly named hydrargyrum (/haɪˈdrɑːrdʒərəm/). A heavy, silvery d-block element, mercury is the only metallic element that is liquid at standard conditions for temperature and pressure; the only other element that is liquid under these conditions is bromine, though metals such as caesium, gallium, and rubidium melt just above room temperature.

name: Thallium

image.title: 8 grams pure thallium under argon. Original size in cm: 0.7 x 1.5
image.attribution: Hi-Res Images ofChemical Elements, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, source: https://images-of-elements.com/thallium.php

funFacts: Thallium poisoning was the preferred method of the fictional detective Agatha Christie's murderers — it causes hair loss before death.
Thallium was once used as a rat poison and depilatory (hair remover) before its extreme toxicity was fully understood.
Thallium-201 is used in nuclear medicine to evaluate blood flow to the heart muscle.
summary: Thallium is a chemical element with symbol Tl and atomic number 81. This soft gray post-transition metal is not found free in nature. When isolated, it resembles tin, but discolors when exposed to air.
discoveryCountry: United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland

name: Lead

image.title: Ultrapure Lead Bead from two sides. Original size in cm: 1.5 x 2
image.attribution: Chemical Elements, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, source: https://images-of-elements.com/lead.php

funFacts: Lead poisoning from water pipes (plumbum in Latin) contributed to the decline of the Roman Empire — our word 'plumbing' comes from lead.
Leaded gasoline, used worldwide for decades, spread billions of tons of lead into the environment — its removal dramatically reduced crime rates.
Lead is so dense that only 2.5 cm of lead can stop most X-rays — that's why radiologists use lead aprons.
summary: Lead (/lɛd/) is a chemical element in the carbon group with symbol Pb (from Latin:plumbum) and atomic number 82. Lead is a soft, malleable and heavy post-transition metal. Metallic lead has a bluish-white color after being freshly cut, but it soon tarnishes to a dull grayish color when exposed to air.

name: Bismuth

image.title: Bismuth Crystal
image.attribution: Jurii, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, source: https://images-of-elements.com/bismuth.php

funFacts: Bismuth crystals grow in beautiful, rainbow-colored, staircase-shaped formations due to thin oxide layers.
Bismuth subsalicylate is the active ingredient in Pepto-Bismol — bismuth has mild antimicrobial properties.
Bismuth is the heaviest stable element — all heavier elements are radioactive.
summary: Bismuth is a chemical element with symbol Bi and atomic number 83. Bismuth, a pentavalent post-transition metal, chemically resembles arsenic and antimony. Elemental bismuth may occur naturally, although its sulfide and oxide form important commercial ores.

name: Polonium

image.title: This is only an illustration, not polonium itself. A silvery, radioactive metal, producing so much heat that it gets liquid and ionizes the surrounding air
image.attribution: Chemical ELements A Virtual Museum, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0> source: https://images-of-elements.com/polonium.php

funFacts: Polonium was discovered by Marie Curie and named after her homeland, Poland.
Polonium-210 is one of the most toxic substances known — a microgram can kill a person.
Alexander Litvinenko, a former Russian spy, was assassinated in 2006 with polonium-210-laced tea.
summary: Polonium is a chemical element with symbol Po and atomic number 84, discovered in 1898 by Marie Curie and Pierre Curie. A rare and highly radioactive element with no stable isotopes, polonium is chemically similar to bismuth and tellurium, and it occurs in uranium ores. Applications of polonium are few.

name: Astatine

image.title: This is only an illustration, not astatine itself. Crystals similar to iodine, but darker in color than these, which due to the extreme radioactivity glow blue and evaporate to dark purple gas
image.attribution: Chemical ELements A Virtual Museum, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0> source: https://images-of-elements.com/astatine.php

funFacts: Astatine is the rarest naturally occurring element on Earth — at any given time, less than 1 gram exists in the entire crust.
Astatine-211 is being researched for targeted alpha therapy — a precision cancer treatment.
Its name means 'unstable' in Greek, fitting for an element with no stable isotopes and a longest half-life of just 8.1 hours.
summary: Astatine is a very rare radioactive chemical element with the chemical symbol At and atomic number 85. It occurs on Earth as the decay product of various heavier elements. All its isotopes are short-lived; the most stable is astatine-210, with a half-life of 8.1 hours.

name: Radon
appearance: colorless gas, occasionally glows green or red in discharge tubes

image.title: This is only an illustration, not radon itself. Radon is said to glow red in discharge tubes, although it practically is never used for this, due to its strong radioactivity.
image.attribution: Chemical ELements A Virtual Museum, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0> source: https://images-of-elements.com/radon.php

funFacts: Radon is the leading cause of lung cancer among non-smokers and seeps into homes through foundations from natural uranium decay.
Radon gas, being the heaviest noble gas, accumulates in the lowest levels of buildings.
Short-lived radon daughters that cling to dust particles are what actually cause lung damage, not radon itself.
summary: Radon is a chemical element with symbol Rn and atomic number 86. It is a radioactive, colorless, odorless, tasteless noble gas, occurring naturally as a decay product of radium. Its most stable isotope, 222Rn, has a half-life of 3.8 days.

name: Francium

image.title: This is only an illustration, not francium itself.
image.attribution: Chemical ELements A Virtual Museum, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0> source: https://images-of-elements.com/francium.jpg

funFacts: Francium is the most unstable naturally occurring element — its longest-lived isotope has a half-life of only 22 minutes.
At any given time, there is estimated to be less than 1 ounce of francium in the entire Earth's crust.
Francium is so radioactive that if you could gather a visible sample, it would instantly vaporize from its own heat.
summary: Francium is a chemical element with symbol Fr and atomic number 87. It used to be known as eka-caesium and actinium K. It is the second-least electronegative element, behind only caesium. Francium is a highly radioactive metal that decays into astatine, radium, and radon.

name: Radium

image.title: Radium electroplated on a very small sample of copper foil and covered with polyurethane to prevent reaction with the air
image.attribution: grenadier, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

funFacts: Marie Curie, who discovered radium, died of aplastic anemia caused by decades of radiation exposure from her research.
Radium was once added to watch dials, toothpaste, and health tonics — before the devastating health effects were understood.
Radium glows faintly blue-green in the dark due to ionization of surrounding air.
summary: Radium is a chemical element with symbol Ra and atomic number 88. It is the sixth element in group 2 of the periodic table, also known as the alkaline earth metals. Pure radium is almost colorless, but it readily combines with nitrogen (rather than oxygen) on exposure to air, forming a black surface layer of radium nitride (Ra3N2).

name: Actinium

image.title: Actinium-225 medical radioisotope held in a v-vial at ORNL. The blue glow comes from the ionization of surrounding air by alpha particles
image.attribution: Oak Ridge National Laboratory, CC BY 2.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.0>, via Wikimedia Commons, source: https://www.flickr.com/photos/oakridgelab/31481701837/
