funFacts: Actinium is so radioactive it glows blue in the dark from ionizing the surrounding air.
Actinium-225 is being studied as a cancer treatment — it can be targeted to tumor cells to deliver alpha radiation.
Actinium gave its name to the actinide series of elements, all of which are radioactive.
summary: Actinium is a radioactive chemical element with symbol Ac (not to be confused with the abbreviation for an acetyl group) and atomic number 89, which was discovered in 1899. It was the first non-primordial radioactive element to be isolated. Polonium, radium and radon were observed before actinium, but they were not isolated until 1902.

name: Thorium

image.title: Thorium Metal in Ampoule, corroded
image.attribution: W. Oelen, CC BY-SA 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

funFacts: Thorium is 3–4 times more abundant than uranium in Earth's crust and could theoretically fuel reactors with less waste.
Old gas lantern mantles contained thorium oxide — they glowed brilliantly because thorium is a fine incandescent material.
Thorium reactors were tested in the 1960s and are now being revived as a potentially safer nuclear energy technology.
summary: Thorium is a chemical element with symbol Th and atomic number 90. A radioactive actinide metal, thorium is one of only two significantly radioactive elements that still occur naturally in large quantities as a primordial element (the other being uranium). It was discovered in 1828 by the Norwegian Reverend and amateur mineralogist Morten Thrane Esmark and identified by the Swedish chemist Jöns Jakob Berzelius, who named it after Thor, the Norse god of thunder.

name: Protactinium

image.title: This sample of Protactinium-233 (dark circular area in the photo) was photographed in the light from its own radioactive emission (the lighter area) at the National Reactor Testing Station in Idaho.
image.attribution: ENERGY.GOV, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

funFacts: Protactinium is one of the rarest and most expensive naturally occurring elements to isolate.
Its most stable isotope, Pa-231, has a half-life of 32,760 years and is a decay product of uranium-235.
Protactinium was independently discovered by three different research groups between 1913 and 1918.
summary: Protactinium is a chemical element with symbol Pa and atomic number 91. It is a dense, silvery-gray metal which readily reacts with oxygen, water vapor and inorganic acids. It forms various chemical compounds where protactinium is usually present in the oxidation state +5, but can also assume +4 and even +2 or +3 states.

name: Uranium

image.title: A biscuit of uranium metal after reduction via the Ames Process. c.1943.
image.attribution: Unknown authorUnknown author, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

funFacts: Uranium was used as a yellow-orange ceramic glaze for pottery — Fiesta Ware — before nuclear fears ended the practice.
Natural uranium is weakly radioactive — a typical rock contains more radioactivity per kilogram than uranium ore.
The energy in 1 kg of enriched uranium equals burning about 3,000 tons of coal.
summary: Uranium is a chemical element with symbol U and atomic number 92. It is a silvery-white metal in the actinide series of the periodic table. A uranium atom has 92 protons and 92 electrons, of which 6 are valence electrons.

name: Neptunium

image.title: Neptunium 237 sphere (6 kg)
image.attribution: Los Alamos National Laboratory,, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

funFacts: Neptunium was the first transuranium element to be synthesized, in 1940 at Berkeley.
Neptunium-237 has a half-life of 2.14 million years and is a significant component of nuclear waste.
It was named after Neptune, the planet beyond Uranus — just as neptunium is beyond uranium in the periodic table.
summary: Neptunium is a chemical element with symbol Np and atomic number 93. A radioactive actinide metal, neptunium is the first transuranic element. Its position in the periodic table just after uranium, named after the planet Uranus, led to it being named after Neptune, the next planet beyond Uranus.

name: Plutonium
appearance: silvery white, tarnishing to dark gray in air

image.title: Plutonium Ring
image.attribution: Los Alamos National Laboratory, Attribution, via Wikimedia Commons

funFacts: The plutonium bomb dropped on Nagasaki required only 6.2 kg of plutonium — less than the size of a softball.
Plutonium-238 is used in radioisotope thermoelectric generators (RTGs) to power spacecraft like Voyager and Cassini.
Plutonium is pyrophoric — small pieces can spontaneously ignite in air at room temperature.
summary: Plutonium is a transuranic radioactive chemical element with symbol Pu and atomic number 94. It is an actinide metal of silvery-gray appearance that tarnishes when exposed to air, and forms a dull coating when oxidized. The element normally exhibits six allotropes and four oxidation states.

name: Americium

image.title: A small disc of Am-241 under the microscope.
image.attribution: Bionerd, CC BY 3.0 <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0>, via Wikimedia Commons

funFacts: Americium-241 is found in virtually every smoke detector — ionizing radiation from americium detects smoke particles.
Americium is the only synthetic element found in most people's homes.
Americium was first produced in 1944 as part of the Manhattan Project.
summary: Americium is a radioactive transuranic chemical element with symbol Am and atomic number 95. This member of the actinide series is located in the periodic table under the lanthanide element europium, and thus by analogy was named after the Americas. Americium was first produced in 1944 by the group of Glenn T.Seaborg from Berkeley, California, at the metallurgical laboratory of University of Chicago.

name: Curium
appearance: silvery metallic, glows purple in the dark

image.title: A piece of curium, which emitts strong radiation that makes it glow
image.attribution: European Union, The Actinide Group, Institute for Transuranium Elements (JRC-ITU), source: https://images-of-elements.com/curium.php

funFacts: Curium was named in honor of Marie and Pierre Curie, recognizing their pioneering work on radioactivity.
Curium is intensely radioactive — it glows red-hot spontaneously from its own radioactive decay.
Curium-244 was used as an alpha-particle source in X-ray spectrometers sent to Mars.
summary: Curium is a transuranic radioactive chemical element with symbol Cm and atomic number 96. This element of the actinide series was named after Marie and Pierre Curie – both were known for their research on radioactivity. Curium was first intentionally produced and identified in July 1944 by the group of Glenn T. Seaborg at the University of California, Berkeley.

name: Berkelium

image.title: It took 250 days to make enough berkelium, shown here (in dissolved state), to synthesize element 117
image.attribution: ORNL, Department of Energy, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

funFacts: Berkelium is named after Berkeley, California, where it was synthesized at the University of California.
Only microgram quantities of berkelium have ever been produced — it's incredibly difficult and expensive to make.
Berkelium-249 is used as a target material to create even heavier elements like tennessine.
summary: Berkelium is a transuranic radioactive chemical element with symbol Bk and atomic number 97. It is a member of the actinide and transuranium element series. It is named after the city of Berkeley, California, the location of the University of California Radiation Laboratory where it was discovered in December 1949.

name: Californium

image.title: A disc of californium metal (249Cf, 10 mg). The source implies that the disc has a diameter about twice the thickness of a typical pin, or on the order of 1 mm
image.attribution: United States Department of Energy (see File:Einsteinium.jpg), Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

funFacts: Californium-252 is one of the few portable neutron sources — it's used to start nuclear reactors and detect landmines.
Californium is so rare that 1 microgram costs about $27, making it one of the most expensive materials on Earth.
Californium's neutron emission is used in cancer treatment through a technique called neutron capture therapy.
summary: Californium is a radioactive metallic chemical element with symbol Cf and atomic number 98. The element was first made in 1950 at the University of California Radiation Laboratory in Berkeley, by bombarding curium with alpha particles (helium-4 ions). It is an actinide element, the sixth transuranium element to be synthesized, and has the second-highest atomic mass of all the elements that have been produced in amounts large enough to see with the unaided eye (after einsteinium).

name: Einsteinium

image.title: 300 micrograms of Einsteinium 253, which has a half-life of 20 days.
image.attribution: Haire, R. G., US Department of Energy.Touched up by Materialscientist at en.wikipedia., Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

funFacts: Einsteinium was discovered in the fallout from the first hydrogen bomb test (Ivy Mike) in 1952.
Einsteinium was secretly studied for years before its existence was publicly announced.
Only nanogram quantities of einsteinium have ever been produced — studying its chemistry requires extraordinary sensitivity.
summary: Einsteinium is a synthetic element with symbol Es and atomic number 99. It is the seventh transuranic element, and an actinide. Einsteinium was discovered as a component of the debris of the first hydrogen bomb explosion in 1952, and named after Albert Einstein.

name: Fermium

image.title: Fermium was first observed in the fallout from the Ivy Mike nuclear test.
image.attribution: U.S. Department of Energy, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

funFacts: Fermium was also discovered in hydrogen bomb fallout in 1952, named after Enrico Fermi.
No weighable amount of fermium has ever been produced — it exists only atom by atom.
Fermium-257 has a half-life of about 100 days — one of the longest among fermium isotopes.
summary: Fermium is a synthetic element with symbol Fm and atomic number 100. It is a member of the actinide series. It is the heaviest element that can be formed by neutron bombardment of lighter elements, and hence the last element that can be prepared in macroscopic quantities, although pure fermium metal has not yet been prepared.

name: Mendelevium

image.title: This is only an illustration, not mendelevium itself. Chemically similar to Thulium, the highly radioactive heavy metal emits very energetic α-radiation.
image.attribution: Chemical Elements A Virtual Museum under a Creative Commons Attribution 3.0 Unported License, source: https://images-of-elements.com/mendelevium.php

funFacts: Mendelevium was the first element to be produced one atom at a time — scientists confirmed its existence by detecting single atoms.
It was named after Dmitri Mendeleev, creator of the periodic table.
Mendelevium was synthesized by bombarding einsteinium with alpha particles — itself an incredibly rare element.
summary: Mendelevium is a synthetic element with chemical symbol Md (formerly Mv) and atomic number 101. A metallic radioactive transuranic element in the actinide series, it is the first element that currently cannot be produced in macroscopic quantities through neutron bombardment of lighter elements. It is the antepenultimate actinide and the ninth transuranic element.

name: Nobelium

image.title: This is only an illustration, not nobelium itself. Nobelium can only be made in very small amounts and emits strong radiation of various kinds.
image.attribution: Chemical Elements A Virtual Museum under a Creative Commons Attribution 3.0 Unported License, source: https://images-of-elements.com/nobelium.php

funFacts: Nobelium was named after Alfred Nobel, the inventor of dynamite and founder of the Nobel Prizes.
There was significant controversy over nobelium's discovery — the Soviet Union and Sweden disputed credit for years.
Nobelium-259 has the longest known half-life for nobelium isotopes — about 58 minutes.
summary: Nobelium is a synthetic chemical element with symbol No and atomic number 102. It is named in honor of Alfred Nobel, the inventor of dynamite and benefactor of science. A radioactive metal, it is the tenth transuranic element and is the penultimate member of the actinide series.

name: Lawrencium

image.title: This is only an illustration, not lawrencium itself. Lawrencium can only be made in very small amounts and emits strong radiation
image.attribution: Chemical Elements A Virtual Museum under a Creative Commons Attribution 3.0 Unported License, source: https://images-of-elements.com/lawrencium.php

funFacts: Lawrencium is named after Ernest Lawrence, the inventor of the cyclotron — the machine used to create many transuranium elements.
Lawrencium is the last member of the actinide series.
Lawrencium was first produced in 1961 by bombarding californium with boron ions.
summary: Lawrencium is a synthetic chemical element with chemical symbol Lr (formerly Lw) and atomic number 103. It is named in honor of Ernest Lawrence, inventor of the cyclotron, a device that was used to discover many artificial radioactive elements. A radioactive metal, lawrencium is the eleventh transuranic element and is also the final member of the actinide series.

name: Rutherfordium

image.title: Decay traces in a spark chamber, not of rutherfordium, but of a pion. This is a completely different, unrelated particle, but the decay of rutherfordium would make streaks there, too.
image.attribution: Image © CERN, Chemical Elements A Virtual Museum under a Creative Commons Attribution 3.0 Unported License, source: https://images-of-elements.com/rutherfordium.php

funFacts: Rutherfordium is named after Ernest Rutherford, who discovered the atomic nucleus.
Rutherfordium was the first transactinide element and the subject of a naming dispute between the US and Soviet Union for decades.
Its chemistry has been studied only a few atoms at a time — it behaves similarly to hafnium in Group 4.
summary: Rutherfordium is a chemical element with symbol Rf and atomic number 104, named in honor of physicist Ernest Rutherford. It is a synthetic element (an element that can be created in a laboratory but is not found in nature) and radioactive; the most stable known isotope, 267Rf, has a half-life of approximately 1.3 hours. In the periodic table of the elements, it is a d - block element and the second of the fourth - row transition elements.

name: Dubnium

image.title: No Image Found
image.attribution: Chemical Elements A Virtual Museum under a Creative Commons Attribution 3.0 Unported License, source: https://images-of-elements.com/dubnium.php

funFacts: Dubnium was named after Dubna, Russia, home of the Joint Institute for Nuclear Research where it was synthesized.
The name 'hahnium' was used by American scientists and 'nielsbohrium' by others — IUPAC settled on dubnium in 1997.
Dubnium's most stable isotope has a half-life of only 28 hours.
summary: Dubnium is a chemical element with symbol Db and atomic number 105. It is named after the town of Dubna in Russia (north of Moscow), where it was first produced. It is a synthetic element (an element that can be created in a laboratory but is not found in nature) and radioactive; the most stable known isotope, dubnium-268, has a half-life of approximately 28 hours.

name: Seaborgium

image.title: No Image Found
image.attribution: Chemical Elements A Virtual Museum under a Creative Commons Attribution 3.0 Unported License, source: https://images-of-elements.com/seaborgium.php

funFacts: Seaborgium is one of the few elements named after a living person — Glenn Seaborg was alive when it was officially named.
Glenn Seaborg joked that he was the only person who could write his address in periodic table symbols: Sg, Lr, Bk, Cf, Am.
Seaborgium has been shown to behave chemically like tungsten, consistent with its position in Group 6.
summary: Seaborgium is a synthetic element with symbol Sg and atomic number 106. Its most stable isotope 271Sg has a half-life of 1.9 minutes. A more recently discovered isotope 269Sg has a potentially slightly longer half-life (ca.

name: Bohrium

image.title: No Image Found
image.attribution: Chemical Elements A Virtual Museum under a Creative Commons Attribution 3.0 Unported License, source: https://images-of-elements.com/bohrium.php

funFacts: Bohrium is named after Niels Bohr, the Danish physicist who developed the Bohr model of the atom.
Bohrium-270 has a half-life of about 61 seconds — relatively long for superheavy elements.
Only a handful of atoms of bohrium have ever been produced.
summary: Bohrium is a chemical element with symbol Bh and atomic number 107. It is named after Danish physicist Niels Bohr. It is a synthetic element (an element that can be created in a laboratory but is not found in nature) and radioactive; the most stable known isotope, 270Bh, has a half-life of approximately 61 seconds.

name: Hassium
discoverer: GSI Helmholtz Centre for Heavy Ion Research

image.title: No Image Found
image.attribution: Chemical Elements A Virtual Museum under a Creative Commons Attribution 3.0 Unported License, source: https://images-of-elements.com/hassium.php

funFacts: Hassium is named after the German state of Hesse, where the GSI Helmholtz Centre discovered it.
Hassium oxide (HsO₄) was studied and found to behave similarly to osmium tetroxide, confirming periodic trends.
Hassium was synthesized by bombarding lead with iron ions in 1984.
summary: Hassium is a chemical element with symbol Hs and atomic number 108, named after the German state of Hesse. It is a synthetic element (an element that can be created in a laboratory but is not found in nature) and radioactive; the most stable known isotope, 269Hs, has a half-life of approximately 9.7 seconds, although an unconfirmed metastable state, 277mHs, may have a longer half-life of about 130 seconds. More than 100 atoms of hassium have been synthesized to date.

name: Meitnerium
discoverer: GSI Helmholtz Centre for Heavy Ion Research

image.title: No Image Found
image.attribution: Chemical Elements A Virtual Museum under a Creative Commons Attribution 3.0 Unported License, source: https://images-of-elements.com/meitnerium.php

funFacts: Meitnerium is named after Lise Meitner, who co-discovered nuclear fission — making her one of the few women honored with an element name.
Lise Meitner was controversially overlooked for the Nobel Prize that was awarded to her collaborator Otto Hahn alone.
No chemical properties of meitnerium have been observed — it exists for less than a second.
summary: Meitnerium is a chemical element with symbol Mt and atomic number 109. It is an extremely radioactive synthetic element (an element not found in nature that can be created in a laboratory). The most stable known isotope, meitnerium-278, has a half-life of 7.6 seconds.

name: Darmstadtium
discoverer: GSI Helmholtz Centre for Heavy Ion Research

image.title: No Image Found
image.attribution: Chemical Elements A Virtual Museum under a Creative Commons Attribution 3.0 Unported License, source: https://images-of-elements.com/darmstadtium.php

funFacts: Darmstadtium is named after Darmstadt, Germany, where the GSI particle accelerator facility is located.
Its most stable isotope, Ds-281, has a half-life of about 12.7 seconds.
Darmstadtium was synthesized in 1994 using a nickel beam fired at lead targets.
summary: Darmstadtium is a chemical element with symbol Ds and atomic number 110. It is an extremely radioactive synthetic element. The most stable known isotope, darmstadtium-281, has a half-life of approximately 10 seconds.

name: Roentgenium
discoverer: GSI Helmholtz Centre for Heavy Ion Research

image.title: No Image Found
image.attribution: Chemical Elements A Virtual Museum under a Creative Commons Attribution 3.0 Unported License, source: https://images-of-elements.com/roentgenium.php

funFacts: Roentgenium is named after Wilhelm Röntgen, who discovered X-rays in 1895.
Only a few atoms of roentgenium have been produced — each one decaying within seconds.
Roentgenium is predicted to be a solid metal with unusual chemical properties due to relativistic effects.
summary: Roentgenium is a chemical element with symbol Rg and atomic number 111. It is an extremely radioactive synthetic element (an element that can be created in a laboratory but is not found in nature); the most stable known isotope, roentgenium-282, has a half-life of 2.1 minutes. Roentgenium was first created in 1994 by the GSI Helmholtz Centre for Heavy Ion Research near Darmstadt, Germany.

name: Copernicium
discoverer: GSI Helmholtz Centre for Heavy Ion Research

image.title: No Image Found
image.attribution: Chemical Elements A Virtual Museum under a Creative Commons Attribution 3.0 Unported License, source: https://images-of-elements.com/copernicium.php

funFacts: Copernicium is named after astronomer Nicolaus Copernicus, who first proposed a heliocentric solar system.
Copernicium is predicted to be a gas at room temperature — the only metal element expected to behave as a noble gas.
Relativistic effects in copernicium are so extreme that its electrons move at close to the speed of light.
summary: Copernicium is a chemical element with symbol Cn and atomic number 112. It is an extremely radioactive synthetic element that can only be created in a laboratory. The most stable known isotope, copernicium-285, has a half-life of approximately 29 seconds, but it is possible that this copernicium isotope may have a nuclear isomer with a longer half-life, 8.9 min.

name: Nihonium

image.title: No Image Found
image.attribution: Chemical Elements A Virtual Museum under a Creative Commons Attribution 3.0 Unported License, source: https://images-of-elements.com/nihonium.php

funFacts: Nihonium is the first element discovered in Asia — its name comes from 'Nihon,' the Japanese word for Japan.
Japanese scientists at RIKEN worked for nearly a decade, from 2004 to 2012, to synthesize and confirm nihonium.
Nihonium's most stable isotope has a half-life of about 10 seconds.
summary: Nihonium is a chemical element with atomic number 113. It has a symbol Nh. It is a synthetic element (an element that can be created in a laboratory but is not found in nature) and is extremely radioactive; its most stable known isotope, nihonium-286, has a half-life of 20 seconds.

name: Flerovium

image.title: No Image Found
image.attribution: Chemical Elements A Virtual Museum under a Creative Commons Attribution 3.0 Unported License, source: https://images-of-elements.com/flerovium.php

funFacts: Flerovium is named after the Flerov Laboratory of Nuclear Reactions in Dubna, Russia.
Flerovium is predicted to be a volatile metal or even a noble gas at room temperature due to relativistic effects.
Only a few atoms of flerovium have ever been created — each lasting less than a second.
summary: Flerovium is a superheavy artificial chemical element with symbol Fl and atomic number 114. It is an extremely radioactive synthetic element. The element is named after the Flerov Laboratory of Nuclear Reactions of the Joint Institute for Nuclear Research in Dubna, Russia, where the element was discovered in 1998.
etymology: Named after Flerov Laboratory of Nuclear Reactions

name: Moscovium

image.title: No Image Found
image.attribution: Chemical Elements A Virtual Museum under a Creative Commons Attribution 3.0 Unported License, source: https://images-of-elements.com/moscovium.php

funFacts: Moscovium is named after Moscow Oblast, where the Joint Institute for Nuclear Research in Dubna is located.
Moscovium was discovered in 2003 by a Russian-American collaboration.
Its most stable isotope decays in about 220 milliseconds.
summary: Moscovium is the name of a synthetic superheavy element in the periodic table that has the symbol Mc and has the atomic number 115. It is an extremely radioactive element; its most stable known isotope, moscovium-289, has a half-life of only 220 milliseconds. It is also known as eka-bismuth or simply element 115.

name: Livermorium

image.title: No Image Found
image.attribution: Chemical Elements A Virtual Museum under a Creative Commons Attribution 3.0 Unported License, source: https://images-of-elements.com/livermorium.php

funFacts: Livermorium is named after the Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory in California.
Livermorium was synthesized by the same Russian-American collaboration that discovered moscovium.
Livermorium atoms exist for only tens of milliseconds before decaying.
summary: Livermorium is a synthetic superheavy element with symbol Lv and atomic number 116. It is an extremely radioactive element that has only been created in the laboratory and has not been observed in nature. The element is named after the Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory in the United States, which collaborated with the Joint Institute for Nuclear Research in Dubna, Russia to discover livermorium in 2000.
etymology: Named after Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory

name: Tennessine

image.title: No Image Found
image.attribution: Chemical Elements A Virtual Museum under a Creative Commons Attribution 3.0 Unported License, source: https://images-of-elements.com/tenessine.php

funFacts: Tennessine is named after Tennessee, recognizing the contributions of Oak Ridge National Laboratory, Vanderbilt University, and the University of Tennessee.
Tennessine was first synthesized in 2010 by a Russian-American team.
With an atomic number of 117, tennessine filled the last remaining gap in the seventh period of the periodic table.
summary: Tennessine is a superheavy artificial chemical element with an atomic number of 117 and a symbol of Ts. Also known as eka-astatine or element 117, it is the second-heaviest known element and penultimate element of the 7th period of the periodic table. As of 2016, fifteen tennessine atoms have been observed: six when it was first synthesized in 2010, seven in 2012, and two in 2014.

name: Oganesson

image.title: No Image Found
image.attribution: Chemical Elements A Virtual Museum under a Creative Commons Attribution 3.0 Unported License, source: https://images-of-elements.com/oganesson.php

funFacts: Oganesson is named after Yuri Oganessian, a living nuclear physicist — one of the few elements named after a living person.
Oganesson completes the seventh row of the periodic table — it should be a noble gas, but relativistic effects may make it reactive.
Only 5 atoms of oganesson have ever been confirmed, each lasting less than a millisecond.
summary: Oganesson is IUPAC's name for the transactinide element with the atomic number 118 and element symbol Og. It is also known as eka-radon or element 118, and on the periodic table of the elements it is a p-block element and the last one of the 7th period. Oganesson is currently the only synthetic member of group 18.
